name: Kibisis
description: The Kibisis is a special bag or pouch featured prominently in the myth of Perseus. It was a magical, expandable sack given to Perseus by the nymphs (or sometimes the Graeae) to safely contain the severed head of the Gorgon Medusa. The Kibisis was crucial because Medusa's gaze could still turn anyone who looked upon it to stone, even after her death. Therefore, the bag needed to completely conceal the head and prevent its power from affecting Perseus or anyone nearby. The Kibisis was typically described as being made of strong, protective materials, possibly imbued with magical properties to further nullify Medusa's petrifying gaze. Its expandable nature allowed it to accommodate the head regardless of its size or any residual magical energy emanating from it. After Perseus completed his mission and no longer needed the head, he returned the Kibisis to Hermes, who then returned it to Athena. Athena then used the Kibisis to carry and protect the Gorgon's head, which she eventually affixed to her shield, the Aegis, to instill fear in her enemies. The Kibisis represents ingenuity, divine assistance, and the careful management of dangerous power.
subtitle: The magical sack that held Medusa's head.
origin: Gift from the nymphs or Graeae to Perseus

keyMyths.title: Perseus and Medusa
keyMyths.description: Perseus used the Kibisis to safely transport Medusa's head after beheading her, preventing its petrifying gaze from harming others. The Kibisis enabled him to complete his quest without being turned to stone.

keyMyths.title: Athena's Aegis
keyMyths.description: Athena eventually received the Kibisis and its contents. She then fixed Medusa's head onto her shield, the Aegis, utilizing its power for protection and intimidation in battle.

sources.author: Apollodorus
sources.description: A comprehensive source of Greek myths and legends.
sources.text: A comprehensive source of Greek myths and legends.
